stories.headline: 3,800 JBS Workers Strike at Greeley Beef Plant Over Wages and Healthcare
stories.summary:
About 3,800 workers at the JBS beef processing plant in Greeley walked off the job Monday, launching what union officials say is the first strike at a U.S. beef slaughterhouse since the 1980s. Members of UFCW Local 7 say negotiations have been ongoing since July but have reached a stalemate over pay and healthcare costs.

Union officials accused JBS of offering less than 2% in average annual wage increases — well below the rate of inflation in Colorado — while also shifting rising healthcare costs onto workers. JBS countered that many employees reported for work despite the walkout.

The Colorado Cattlemen's Association noted that any disruption at a plant of this scale will ripple through the cattle marketplace, affecting producers and feedlots across the region. Union representatives said the strike could last for weeks.

stories.headline: Universities Sue Trump Administration to Block Dismantling of Boulder's NCAR
stories.summary:
The University Corporation for Atmospheric Research, a nonprofit consortium of 129 North American universities, filed a federal lawsuit Monday to halt the Trump administration's planned reorganization of the National Center for Atmospheric Research in Boulder. The lawsuit was filed in Colorado federal court and names several federal agencies and their leaders as defendants.

The suit alleges the administration's effort to dismantle NCAR is part of a "campaign of retaliation" against Colorado for maintaining mail-in voting and refusing to grant clemency to former county clerk and Trump ally Tina Peters, who was convicted of felonies in an election misconduct case. Since December, the administration has sought to transfer management of NCAR's supercomputer, canceled millions in climate research funding, imposed gag orders on staff, and proposed selling the center's iconic Boulder headquarters.

NCar employs approximately 820 people, about half of whom live and work in the Boulder area. The center's research underpins weather forecasting and atmospheric science relied upon by aviation, agriculture, shipping, and national security agencies. UCAR leaders said the lawsuit aims to prevent further unlawful federal action against the institution.

stories.headline: Denver Crosswalk Audio Signals Hacked to Broadcast Anti-Trump Messages
stories.summary:
Several crosswalk audio signals along East Colfax Avenue in Denver were hacked over the weekend, broadcasting anti-President Donald Trump messages alongside standard walk signals. Videos circulating on social media captured the robotic voices at intersections including Colfax and Pearl Street and Colfax and Washington Street delivering expletive-laden political statements.

Denver Department of Transportation and Infrastructure spokesperson Nancy Kuhn said the affected crosswalk push-buttons had been newly installed and were not yet officially operational. The devices still had factory-default passwords that, Kuhn acknowledged, are easily found online — a vulnerability that allowed the hack.

City officials changed the passwords and said they do not expect the situation to recur at those locations. The Denver Police Department did not immediately respond to questions about whether a suspect had been identified or whether charges would be filed.

stories.headline: Penguins Rout Avalanche 7-2 at Ball Arena as Malkin Returns from Suspension
stories.summary:
The Pittsburgh Penguins defeated the Colorado Avalanche 7-2 at Ball Arena on Monday night, handing Colorado its second consecutive loss. Evgeni Malkin, returning from a five-game suspension, scored two goals and added an assist to lead Pittsburgh's offense.

Colorado starter Scott Wedgewood was pulled after allowing three goals on just five shots in the opening 13 minutes. Mackenzie Blackwood stopped 12 shots in relief, but the Penguins controlled the game from start to finish, scoring five goals on their first ten shots in the second period alone. Nathan MacKinnon and Brent Burns scored for the Avalanche.

The loss dropped Colorado to 44-13-9 on the season. The Avalanche have now lost two straight and three of their last four games heading into the final stretch of the regular season.

stories.headline: Historic March Heat Wave Forecast for Denver This Week, Records Expected to Fall
stories.summary:
Denver is forecast to experience an unprecedented March heat wave beginning Tuesday, with meteorologists predicting highs around 71 degrees on St. Patrick's Day and near or above 80 degrees for four consecutive days later in the week. The National Weather Service says the stretch could be the hottest and longest-lasting March heat wave in recorded history for Colorado.

Denver's all-time warmest March temperature of 84 degrees, set on March 26, 1971, could be broken multiple times — potentially as early as Friday. There is also a chance Denver could record its earliest-ever 90-degree day, though 90 degrees is not yet in the official forecast. Daily temperature records on Thursday, Friday, and Saturday are considered likely.

A Red Flag Warning is in effect Tuesday for northeastern Colorado, with wind gusts up to 45 mph and humidity values between 10 and 15 percent raising wildfire concerns. The heat wave follows a weekend snowstorm that left temperatures as low as 5 degrees at Denver International Airport on Monday morning.

stories.headline: Colorado Legislature Eyes AI Chatbot Rules, Data Center Regulations, and Immigration Bills This Week
stories.summary:
The Colorado General Assembly returns from its halfway point this week with a packed agenda that includes first hearings on artificial intelligence chatbot regulations, data center rules, and a pair of immigration enforcement bills. The legislature also awaits Thursday's release of state economic forecasts, which will set the fiscal target for next year's budget amid a projected gap of nearly $1 billion.

The House this week passed Senate Bill 11, requiring large social media companies to comply with law enforcement search warrants more quickly, and Senate Bill 32, aimed at expanding vaccine availability in Colorado. Both bills return to the Senate for consideration of House amendments before heading to Gov. Jared Polis.

Also on the docket are House Bill 1210, which would prohibit companies from using personal data to set individualized prices or wages, and Senate Bill 131, which would ban proposition bets and impose new restrictions on online sports betting. Two immigration bills — House Bill 1275 and House Bill 1276 — will get their first hearings in the House Judiciary Committee, addressing officer mask-wearing and expanded oversight of immigration detention facilities.

stories.headline: Capitol Hill Development Near Governor's Mansion to Include 67 Affordable Apartments
stories.summary:
A state-owned lot adjacent to Colorado's governor's mansion on Capitol Hill is slated to become a six-story building with 67 affordable apartments, according to plans filed with the city. The developer is seeking state funding to support the project.

The development is among several new construction proposals taking shape in Denver's Capitol Hill neighborhood. Separately, plans filed with the city show a 6.7-acre retail center in Denver will be renovated and expanded to accommodate new types of tenants.

The governor's mansion project reflects ongoing efforts by state officials to address Denver's affordable housing shortage by leveraging publicly owned land near the urban core.

stories.headline: Broncos Remain Only NFL Team Without External Free Agent Signing Amid Fan and Media Criticism
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos stand as the only NFL franchise that has not signed a free agent from another team during the current free agency period, drawing widespread criticism from fans and media analysts. The team's offseason inactivity has prompted scrutiny of the front office's strategy heading into the 2026 season.

Denver did sign cornerback Darius McMillian to a restricted free agent tender worth $5.767 million, but that move retained an existing player rather than adding outside talent. McMillian, 25, recorded 56 tackles, 4.0 sacks, nine pass breakups, and two interceptions in 17 games last season and is considered the team's top slot corner.

NFL insiders suggest the Broncos may still be active at certain positions, with tight end remaining a potential area of focus. Whether Denver makes additional moves before the draft remains to be seen.

stories.headline: Spring Break Travel Strains I-70 Corridor and Denver International Airport
stories.summary:
The Colorado Department of Transportation is urging drivers to exercise patience over the next two weeks as spring break brings a surge of out-of-town visitors to mountain destinations, increasing congestion along the Interstate 70 corridor. Sunday's winter weather compounded the situation, with Colorado State Patrol responding to seven crashes on I-70 between mileposts 220 and 250 in Clear Creek and Jefferson counties.

More than 900 flights in and out of Denver International Airport were delayed Sunday, with 141 canceled, according to FlightAware. DIA identified Friday, March 20, and Sunday, March 22, as the busiest upcoming travel days, with peak security lines expected during early morning and afternoon hours.

Adding to the strain, Transportation Security Administration agents are working without pay due to a continued Department of Homeland Security funding lapse. DIA is collecting grocery store and gas station gift cards for TSA workers; as of Friday, 271 cards had been donated. Despite the spring rush, CDOT data shows 58,000 fewer cars passed through Eisenhower Tunnel in January and February compared to the same period last year.

stories.headline: AI-Powered Biohacking Franchise Upgrade Labs to Open First Colorado Location in Boulder
stories.summary:
Upgrade Labs, an artificial intelligence-driven wellness and fitness franchise associated with biohacking entrepreneur Dave Asprey, is set to open its first Colorado location in Boulder on March 27. The franchise offers technology-assisted fitness and recovery services marketed under the "biohacking" wellness category.

The Boulder opening marks the brand's entry into the Colorado market as AI-integrated health and fitness concepts continue to expand nationally. Asprey is widely known for popularizing biohacking practices through his Bulletproof brand and related ventures.

No financial terms of the Boulder franchise agreement were disclosed.

stories.headline: Colorado Education Bills Tackle Teacher Housing, School Vouchers, and More in Second Half of Session
stories.summary:
Colorado lawmakers are advancing several education-focused bills as the second half of the 2026 legislative session gets underway. Senate Bill 139, the Building Excellent Teacher and Employee Residences Act (BETER), would create a grant program to help school districts develop affordable rental housing for staff, converting $40 million in state public school fund interest into $500 million in construction capacity through private investment certificates.

A separate debate centers on a federal tax credit scholarship program that Gov. Jared Polis has indicated Colorado will join. State Democrats are pushing legislation to ensure the program includes inclusion and accountability guardrails, while opponents worry it could establish a de facto school voucher system that diverts resources from public schools.

Other education measures under consideration include proposals addressing school staffing shortages and special education services. With a projected budget shortfall of nearly $1 billion, funding questions loom over all new spending proposals at the Capitol.

stories.headline: Denver St. Patrick's Day Parade, Zoo Events Highlight Metro Weekend Festivities
stories.summary:
Denver's annual St. Patrick's Day parade and a range of zoo exhibitions and regional festivals are drawing residents across the metro area this weekend. The events coincide with the start of an historic warm spell, with temperatures expected to climb into the low 70s on Tuesday, March 17.

The Denver Zoo is among the venues hosting themed programming tied to the holiday weekend. Additional community festivals are scheduled at various locations throughout the metro region.

Organizers are encouraging attendees to plan for large crowds given the combination of the holiday, unusually warm weather, and spring break travel season.

weather.notable: About 16 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: It's amazing that the amount of news that happens in the world every day always just exactly fits the newspaper.

topStory.headline: Israel Claims Killing of Senior Iranian Security Official Ali Larijani; Iran Has Not Confirmed Death
topStory.summary:
Israel announced Tuesday that it had killed Ali Larijani, secretary of Iran's Supreme National Security Council and one of the most senior figures remaining in the Iranian government, in a targeted strike. Israeli Defense Minister Israel Katz made the claim publicly, stating that 'the leaders of the regime are being killed and their capabilities terminated.' Israel's military also announced the killing of Gholamreza Soleimani, commander of Iran's internal Basij militia, in the same operation.

Iran has neither confirmed nor denied Larijani's death as of Tuesday. Iranian state media published a photograph of a handwritten note attributed to Larijani, which also appeared on his social media accounts, though it was unclear whether the note was intended as evidence that he remained alive. The message commemorated 84 Iranian sailors killed in a U.S. strike on a naval vessel and made no reference to the Israeli claim. Larijani was last seen publicly on Friday, attending an Al-Quds Day rally in Tehran alongside President Masoud Pezeshkian.

Larijani has been a central figure in Iranian politics for decades, having previously led Iran's nuclear negotiations with Western powers and served as speaker of parliament. The Israel Defense Forces described him as having 'functioned as the leader of the Iranian regime' following the killing of Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, who died in a U.S.-Israeli strike on February 28, the opening day of the current conflict. The IDF also linked Larijani to the violent suppression of anti-government protests inside Iran.

If confirmed, Larijani's death would represent the most significant targeted killing since Khamenei's death and the start of the war. The claimed strike comes amid an rapidly escalating regional conflict that has drawn in multiple countries. On Tuesday alone, Iran fired 10 ballistic missiles and 45 drones at the United Arab Emirates, air defenses were activated in Qatar's capital Doha, and Israel launched new airstrikes on Tehran. A tanker anchored off the UAE coast was struck by debris, and Dubai International Airport temporarily suspended flights after a drone hit a nearby fuel tank.

Just days before his reported death, Larijani had issued statements calling on Muslim-majority nations to support Iran against what he called 'treacherous aggression' by the United States and Israel, warning Islamic governments that neutrality was incompatible with religious duty. Analysts caution that even if the killing is confirmed, it does not signal the collapse of the Iranian government, which has continued military operations across the region. The broader conflict, now in its third week, has produced strikes across Lebanon, Israel, Iran, Iraq, and the Gulf states.
